summary: Current technology valuations (public and private) are fully valued to stretched — not a bubble like 1999/2000, but more analogous to 2021, with risk of 10-20% public market correction translating to 30-40% drawdowns in high-beta names.
summary_extended: Baker and Gerstner jointly assert that technology valuations — both public and private — are fully valued to stretched following parabolic moves, explicitly comparing the current environment to 2021 rather than the more extreme 1999/2000 bubble. Their reasoning is that the magnitude of recent gains has compressed future return potential and elevated downside risk, without necessarily implying a catastrophic unwind. The specific risk they flag is asymmetric: a normal 10-20% correction in a semiconductor index could translate into 30-40% drawdowns in high-beta names, making position sizing and risk management critical. The investment implication is that both managers are actively selling into secondary market strength to return DPI (distributions to paid-in capital) to LPs, signaling a deliberate de-risking posture. Critically, they stop short of calling a top, but are equally explicit that they are not calling a bottom — a stance of cautious neutrality with a bearish tilt on high-multiple, high-beta tech names.
detail: Baker and Gerstner agree that 'everything in the world of technology is pretty fully valued' after parabolic moves. They explicitly compare to 2021 rather than 1999. A normal semi-index correction of 10-20% could produce 30-40% drawdowns in high-beta names. They are actively selling into secondary market strength to return DPI to LPs. Not calling a top, but explicitly not calling a bottom.
confidence_evidence: Baker and Gerstner both affirm this view explicitly. Gerstner says 'we are selling into this.' Baker agrees valuations are 'extraordinary' (coded language for stretched).
quote_fragment: everything in the world of technology is pretty fully valued
